text.title: cot-basics Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for cot-basics. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Append the CoT trigger phrase ("Let's think step by step.") at the end of the user turn, not in the system prompt.
rule.rationale: Placing the trigger in the system prompt applies CoT to every message, including simple lookups where it adds tokens without benefit. End-of-user-turn placement activates reasoning only for the specific message that needs it.

rule.statement: Always request an explicit answer marker in the prompt (e.g., "End with: Answer: <value>") to separate reasoning from the final answer.
rule.rationale: Without a marker, extracting the final answer from free-form CoT text requires fragile string parsing. An explicit marker makes extraction reliable and model-independent.

rule.statement: Limit few-shot CoT examples to 2–3 high-quality chains. More examples do not always improve accuracy and inflate input tokens.
rule.rationale: Token cost scales linearly with example count. Beyond 3 examples the marginal accuracy gain diminishes while cost continues to increase.

rule.statement: Never include few-shot examples that contain arithmetic or reasoning errors.
rule.rationale: Models imitate the style of examples including mistakes. A single incorrect example can propagate the error pattern to all outputs.

rule.statement: Set temperature ≥ 0.6 when running self-consistency sampling. Temperature=0 collapses all N paths to the same deterministic answer.
rule.rationale: Self-consistency relies on diversity across N reasoning paths. Without temperature variation, all paths are identical and voting provides no benefit.

rule.statement: When self-consistency confidence is below 0.6, route to human review rather than propagating the majority answer.
rule.rationale: Low confidence means the reasoning paths disagree substantially. A majority answer with confidence 0.4 is only marginally better than a random guess in high-stakes contexts.

rule.statement: Prefer Claude extended thinking (budget_tokens) over manual CoT prompting when accuracy is paramount.
rule.rationale: Extended thinking uses a private scratchpad, avoids the parsing overhead of extracting answers from free-form text, and does not require few-shot examples.

rule.statement: Decompose a CoT agent system into three roles: reasoner (emits reasoning + answer), judge (checks consistency), extractor (parses final answer reliably).
rule.rationale: This separation of concerns allows the reasoner to optimize for depth of analysis, the judge to catch errors early, and the extractor to use the explicit answer marker rather than fragile heuristics.

rule.statement: When testing CoT prompts locally, use the `llm` command-line tool (Simon Willison) for quick iteration against Claude, OpenAI, or local models.
rule.rationale: The `llm` tool eliminates Python boilerplate for one-off CoT experiments, supports streaming, and caches credentials for multiple providers in one config file.
